name: CookJunkie
tagline: Rebuilt a 15,000-recipe WordPress site with AI-repaired data, a social recipe community, and a Stripe + Lulu cookbook business on top.
problem: A 15,000-recipe site had outgrown WordPress: slow pages, years of malformed and inconsistent data, and constant scraper traffic from competitor services. The owner wanted it faster and cleaner — and wanted to launch a print-on-demand cookbook business plus a social recipe community on top of it.

actions.title: Migrated 15,000 recipes without breaking a single URL
actions.body: The old WordPress site was slow and straining. Moved the entire corpus onto modern infrastructure while preserving every trailing-slash and taxonomy-prefix URL byte-for-byte, so organic search didn't take a hit during the cutover.

actions.title: Repaired years of messy recipe data with an AI-in-the-loop pipeline
actions.body: Fifteen thousand recipes meant fifteen thousand chances for missing ingredients, malformed yields, and inconsistent formatting. Built a pipeline that pulls recipes to per-ID source files, batches them, and fans out to ten Claude agents in parallel — each agent proposes fixes that land back as reviewable diffs, applied via a dry-run-first command. The data is now normalized, consistent, and searchable.

actions.title: Built a social recipe community around the catalog
actions.body: Users create and submit their own recipes, showcase their cooking skills, follow each other, save favorites, and build a pantry that filters the catalog down to what they can actually cook tonight. Community on top of the existing library, not in a separate silo.

actions.title: Shipped an AI chat assistant with user context and site-native tools
actions.body: A chat window that knows who's using it — pulling in the logged-in user's pantry, favorites, skills, and submitted recipes — and exposes site-specific functions the AI can call instead of hallucinating about them. Less chatbot wrapper, more assistant that understands where it's sitting.

actions.title: Shipped a Stripe → Lulu print-on-demand cookbook pipeline
actions.body: Users assemble sharable cookbooks from any recipes in the system; checkout runs through Stripe with no account required. A webhook freezes the rendered PDFs, fires a print job into the Lulu Print API, auto-refunds on Lulu failure, and publishes a tracking page that pulls live status straight from Lulu. Every sharp edge between payment and fulfillment is handled.

actions.title: Auto-posted recipes to Facebook and Instagram on a schedule
actions.body: A scheduled pipeline picks recipes from the catalog, formats them for each network's quirks, and publishes through the Meta Graph API — Facebook and Instagram in one pass. Steady social presence without anyone babysitting a content calendar.

actions.title: Hardened against scrapers and competitor bots
actions.body: A high-value recipe archive is catnip for data-scraper services that resell content to competitors. Layered in rate limits, request-signature checks, and blocklists for known bad actors so the site stops feeding them for free.

outcome: 15,000 recipes live on modern infrastructure with clean, searchable data. Users create and submit recipes, follow each other, save favorites, filter by pantry, and assemble sharable cookbooks — which Stripe and Lulu turn into real printed books. Built and maintained solo.
underTheHood: Next.js 16 + TypeScript + MongoDB, with taxonomy pages that preserve the WordPress-era URL structure to protect SEO. Admin endpoints use bearer auth with machine-readable schemas so external tools don't have to scrape. Sentry for errors, Brevo for transactional mail, R2 for blob storage. No Redis, no queue — idempotency keys and database transactions carry the day.
